sections.text: Evidence means and includes all statements which the court permits or requires to be made before it by witnesses relating to matters of fact under inquiry. Documents produced for inspection are also evidence.
Evidence may be given in any suit or proceeding of the existence or non-existence of every fact in issue and of such other facts as are declared to be relevant.
Facts which form part of the same transaction are relevant whether they occurred at the same time and place or at different times and places.
Facts which are the occasion, cause, or effect of relevant facts are relevant.
Any fact is relevant which shows or constitutes a motive, preparation, or previous or subsequent conduct.
Facts necessary to explain or introduce a fact in issue or relevant fact, or to rebut an inference, or to establish identity, are relevant.
Facts not otherwise relevant are relevant if they are inconsistent with any fact in issue or relevant fact, or if they make the existence of any fact in issue highly probable or improbable.
Facts showing the existence of any state of mind, such as intention, knowledge, good faith, negligence, or rashness, are relevant.
Facts bearing on the question whether an act was accidental or intentional are relevant.
An admission is a statement suggesting any inference as to a fact in issue or relevant fact, made by a party to the proceeding.
Statements made by a party to the proceeding, or by an agent authorized, are admissions.
A confession made by an accused person is irrelevant in criminal proceedings if it appears to have been caused by inducement, threat or promise.
No confession made to a police officer shall be proved against a person accused of any offense.
No confession made by a person while in custody of a police officer shall be proved unless made in the immediate presence of a magistrate.
When any fact is discovered in consequence of information received from a person in custody, so much of the information as relates distinctly to the fact discovered may be proved.
A confession made after the removal of inducement, threat or promise is relevant.

Statements of persons who are dead or cannot be found are relevant when they relate to the cause of death (dying declaration), or are made in the ordinary course of business, or against the pecuniary interest of the maker.
Whoever desires a court to give judgment as to any legal right or liability dependent on the existence of facts which he asserts must prove that those facts exist.
The burden of proof lies on the person who would fail if no evidence were given on either side.
The burden of proof as to any particular fact lies on the person who wishes the court to believe in its existence.
The burden of proving any fact necessary to be proved to make evidence admissible lies on the person who wishes to give such evidence.
The burden of proving the existence of circumstances bringing the case within any exception or proviso is on the accused.
When any fact is especially within the knowledge of any person, the burden of proving that fact is upon him.
When the question is whether a person was alive or dead, and it is shown that he was alive within the last 30 years, the burden of proving his death is on the person who asserts it.
If a person has not been heard of for 7 years by those who would naturally have heard, the burden of proving he is alive is on the person who asserts it.
A child born during the continuance of a valid marriage, or within 280 days after dissolution, shall be conclusive proof of legitimacy.
If a married woman commits suicide within 7 years of marriage and was subjected to cruelty, the court may presume abetment of suicide by the husband.
If a woman's death occurs within 7 years of marriage under unnatural circumstances and she was subjected to cruelty for dowry, the court shall presume dowry death.

When a person has by his declaration, act or omission intentionally caused another to believe a thing to be true and to act upon that belief, neither he nor his representative shall be allowed to deny the truth of that thing.
A tenant of immovable property or a person who came upon land by license is estopped from denying the title of the landlord or licensor.
The acceptor of a bill of exchange is estopped from denying the drawer's authority or the existence of the payee.

All persons shall be competent to testify unless the court considers they are prevented from understanding questions or giving rational answers due to tender years, extreme old age, disease of mind, or any other cause.
Witnesses who cannot speak may give evidence by writing or by signs.
In civil proceedings, the husband or wife of a party is a competent witness.
No judge or magistrate shall be compelled to answer questions about his own conduct in court as a judge or magistrate.
No person who is or has been married shall be compelled to disclose any communication made during marriage.
No person shall be permitted to give evidence derived from unpublished official records relating to affairs of State, except with the permission of the head of the department.
No public officer shall be compelled to disclose communications made to him in official confidence when he considers that the public interest would suffer.
A witness shall not be excused from answering questions on the ground that the answer will incriminate him. However, such answers shall not subject him to arrest or prosecution.
An accomplice shall be a competent witness against an accused person; however, a conviction is not illegal merely because it proceeds on the uncorroborated testimony of an accomplice.
No particular number of witnesses shall be required for the proof of any fact.
The order in which witnesses are produced and examined shall be regulated by the court.
When either party proposes to give evidence of any fact, the judge may ask how the fact is relevant. The judge shall admit the evidence if it is relevant.
The examination of a witness includes examination-in-chief by the party who calls him, cross-examination by the adverse party, and re-examination by the party who called him.
Witnesses shall be first examined-in-chief, then cross-examined, then re-examined. The court may permit cross-examination at any stage.
A leading question is one that suggests the answer desired by the person asking it.
Leading questions may not be asked in examination-in-chief without the court's permission, except on introductory or undisputed matters.
Leading questions may be asked in cross-examination.
A witness may be cross-examined as to previous statements made by him in writing or reduced to writing, without showing the writing to him.
In cross-examination, questions may be put to test the veracity of the witness, to discover his position in life, or to shake his credit.
If a question is asked only to insult or annoy, the court shall forbid it.
The credit of a witness may be impeached by evidence of his unworthiness of belief, or by proof of inconsistent statements.
A witness may refresh his memory by referring to any writing made by himself or under his direction at the time of the transaction.
The contents of documents may be proved either by primary or secondary evidence.
Primary evidence means the document itself produced for the inspection of the court.
Secondary evidence includes certified copies, copies made from the original by mechanical processes, copies made from or compared with the original, and oral accounts of contents.
Documents must be proved by primary evidence except in the cases excepted by the Act.
Secondary evidence may be given when the original is lost, destroyed, in possession of the adversary, or when it is a public document.
Public documents include documents forming the acts or records of sovereign authority, official bodies, tribunals, and public officers.
All documents other than public documents are private documents.
Every person has a right to inspect and obtain certified copies of public documents.
Certified copies may be produced in place of the original public document.
The court shall presume every document purporting to be a certified copy to be genuine if it appears to be a correct copy of the original.
The court shall presume any document purporting to be a record of evidence or judgment to be genuine.
The court shall presume the genuineness of every document purporting to be a Gazette, newspaper, or government publication.
The court shall presume the genuineness of every electronic record purporting to be an official record.
The court shall presume that a document 30 years old (produced from proper custody) is genuine and was signed by the person it purports to be signed by.
Presumption as to abetment of suicide by a married woman: if within 7 years of marriage she commits suicide and was subjected to cruelty, the court may presume the husband abetted suicide.
Presumption as to dowry death: if within 7 years of marriage a woman dies under unnatural circumstances and was subjected to dowry cruelty, the court shall presume dowry death.
